metadata.title: Metastatic Cascade Bottleneck — Why 99.99% of Escaped Cancer Cells Never Succeed

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The funnel floored me: a million cells shed daily, five thousand survive intravasation, and one in a hundred thousand ever forms a macrometastasis, yet that step is where most approved drugs pile in. The argument that earlier, narrower bottlenecks need less drug efficacy to shift outcomes is what I want to test. Help me: (1) explain why a lower-attrition step needs less potency to move survival, (2) contrast the target population size for extravasation versus macrometastasis therapy, and (3) name the most advanced clinical-stage drug hitting an early bottleneck and its endpoint.

props.command: The funnel floored me: a million cells shed daily, five thousand survive intravasation, and one in a hundred thousand ever forms a macrometastasis, yet that step is where most approved drugs pile in. The argument that earlier, narrower bottlenecks need less drug efficacy to shift outcomes is what I want to test. Help me: (1) explain why a lower-attrition step needs less potency to move survival, (2) contrast the target population size for extravasation versus macrometastasis therapy, and (3) name the most advanced clinical-stage drug hitting an early bottleneck and its endpoint.

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. The metastatic cascade — why 99.99 percent of cancer cells that escape never succeed.

A primary tumor sheds a million cells into the bloodstream every day. Almost none of them form metastases. The cells that do succeed kill 90 percent of cancer patients. Metastasis is the most lethal step in cancer — and it fails almost every time it's attempted. The question is what makes the surviving one in a hundred thousand different.
Research the metastatic cascade step by step: quantitative attrition at each step, key molecular bottleneck, and strongest therapeutic strategy targeting each step.

props.command: claude "Research the metastatic cascade step by step: intravasation, survival in circulation, extravasation, micrometastasis formation, dormancy, and outgrowth. For each step, give the quantitative attrition (what fraction of cells survive to the next step), the key molecular bottleneck, and the strongest therapeutic strategy targeting that step."

beats.narration_text: Python script calculating survival rates at each metastatic cascade step.

props.code:
import math
STEPS = [
    ("Shed from primary",      1_000_000, "Tumor vasculature remodeling"),
    ("Survive intravasation",      5_000, "Anoikis resistance"),
    ("Survive circulation",          500, "NK cell evasion, shear stress"),
    ("Extravasate",                   50, "CXCR4-CXCL12 homing"),
    ("Form micromet",                  5, "ECM attachment, dormancy exit"),
    ("Form macrometastasis",           1, "Angiogenic switch"),
]
for i, (step, n, bottleneck) in enumerate(STEPS):
    if i > 0:
        survival = n / STEPS[i-1][1] * 100
        print(f"  -> {survival:.3f}% survive")
    print(f"{step}: ~{n:,}  [{bottleneck}]")

beats.narration_text: Funnel visualization — 10 to the 6 shed per day, 5000 survive intravasation, 500 survive circulation, 50 extravasate, 5 form micromet, 1 in 100000 forms macrometastasis.
For each bottleneck step, what is the most advanced clinical-stage drug or intervention targeting it, its phase, and the primary endpoint being measured?

props.command: claude "For each bottleneck step, what is the most advanced clinical-stage drug or intervention targeting it, its phase, and the primary endpoint being measured?"

beats.narration_text: Same funnel with intervention callout boxes at each bottleneck step.

Most approved therapies target the last step — macrometastasis that is already clinically visible. Every earlier step has a lower attrition rate and a smaller target population, which means a drug needs less efficacy to shift outcomes. Targeting dormancy or extravasation is the uncrowded field.

Read Chambers et al. 2002 in Nature Reviews Cancer — the original quantification of metastatic inefficiency. Then read Liu et al. 2019 in Nature Cell Biology on dormancy reactivation. Find the single molecular event that converts a dormant micromet to an active one — that event is a therapeutic target.
Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
